title: Evaluated Slice 4 robustness report — all 11 criteria met, accepted
notes: Reading a detailed DONE report, cross-referencing against 11 success criteria, checking the orphan cleanup worked. A human PM would need to understand the codebase context and verify claims.

title: Commissioned and reviewed merge of slice/4-robustness to main
notes: Writing merge commission, reviewing merge report, confirming clean fast-forward. Routine but a human still needs to verify.

title: Designed and commissioned Slice 5 — watcher terminal overhaul with token tracking, stakeholder language, session summaries
notes: Detailed UX-level spec for terminal output including layout mockups, business language requirements, token cost tracking design. Required reading existing 716-line watcher, understanding all output paths, designing replacement copy. A human PM/UX person would spend significant time on this spec.

title: Designed estimate-hours skill, created timesheet system, established phase taxonomy and calibration benchmarks
notes: Designing a time-tracking system from scratch: schema, field definitions, phase taxonomy, calibration benchmarks, autonomy requirements, query examples. A human would need to think through the billing model and create the process documentation.

title: Operations Center UX — wireframe (ops-ux-concept.html) and spec (ops-dashboard-spec.md), all 4 screens locked, crew roster added inline, Ziyal role scope defined
notes: Full UX design session: iterated queue panel (inline approval, phase labels), commission detail overlay (two-tab, context-sensitive actions), post-build pipeline (Nog+Bashir coming soon), history rows, health pill, idle states, crew roster (8 agents, active/planned). Also clarified stakeholder-as-bottleneck mental model and defined Ziyal role bounds: lo-fi + written spec, fast sign-off, no visual polish.

title: Per-slice tracking system design — diagnosed null token root cause, designed slicelog schema, watcher integration pattern, Ruflo branch strategy, wrote ADR and handoffs to Kira + O'Brien
notes: Full architecture session: reviewed watcher.js token extraction, found root cause (missing --output-format json in bridge.config.json), designed enforcement gate via DONE frontmatter validation, defined slicelog.jsonl schema, two write points in watcher, Ruflo parallel path. Audited README for gaps. A human architect diagnosing a broken data pipeline, designing the replacement, and writing the ADR + two handoff docs would take 3-4 hours.

title: Answered UX question: should Idle B last-commission text be clickable in Operations Center
notes: Single focused UX judgment call with rationale — a senior designer would spend ~15 min thinking through the affordance and writing a short note. Straightforward but non-trivial opinion work.

title: Answered product question: should Ops Center dashboard be localhost-only or support optional remote access in a future bet
notes: Scoped, single-question product decision with clear rationale. A PM would spend ~15 min thinking through security posture, team size, and build-vs-defer tradeoff, then writing a short decision note.

title: Answered architecture question: should bridge/slicelog.jsonl rotate by month or stay flat forever
notes: Single focused architecture decision — flat vs. rotating log file. A senior architect would spend ~15 min reasoning through current scale, complexity tradeoffs, and deferral logic before writing a short decision record. Straightforward given known bet cadence.

title: Tracking test — add slicelog write-point comments to watcher.js

Processed Dax handoff for Bet 3 per-slice tracking — verified commission 054 accepted and merged, spot-checked all ACs against watcher.js/slicelog.js/config/README, confirmed tracking test responses logged by Dax/Sisko/Ziyal, identified own missing timesheet entry
notes: Reading a multi-part handoff, cross-referencing the ADR, verifying 9 ACs against actual code files, checking git log, auditing timesheet state across 3 roles. A human delivery coordinator would need to understand the full tracking system, read the watcher code, and confirm everything is wired correctly — closer to 1.5h than 0.5h due to the technical verification required.
